item.title: Fundamentals of software development @003
item.content:encoded:
TerminologyвҖӢ

  • Software: A set of statements written in a programming language to perform tasks
  • Statement: A single instruction in a program that performs an action when executed.
  • Snippet: A block of statements.
  • Software Development: The process of creating a software program.
  • OOP: Program composed of interconnected objects at runtime.
  • Expression: An entity-code component of a statement that can be evaluated to produce a value.
  • Assign: The process of storing the result (a value) of one or more expressions.
  • Value: A data item (literal or computed) that is stored in a variable.
  • Compiler: A special program that translates a programming language's source code into machine code.
    • Compilers complete the conversion process all at once after changes are made to the code and before the code is executed
  • Interpreter: A computer program that directly executes code without requiring it to be previously compiled into machine language.
    • Interpreters complete the conversion process one step at a time while the code is being executed.

Software developmentвҖӢ

  • Software development process is an iterative approach.
  • java
    • javac Welcome.java: Compiles the Java source file Welcome.java into class binary file.
    • java Welcome: Executes the Java program Welcome.
  • python
    • python welcome.py: Executes the Python script welcome.py.

OOPвҖӢ

  • Object: An object is a thing, tangible and intangible. An object has fields that contain the data and methods to access and modify the data.
  • Class: A class is an abstract definition of objects. A class is a template of a blueprint that defines what data and methods are included in objects.
  • Method: A block of code grouped together to perform an operation. A method has a name, parameters, and a return type.
  • Field: A field is a data attribute of an object. A field value is exposed using object methods.
  • Organizing code into classes improves modularity, reusability, extendability, and scalability.

Java vs PythonвҖӢ

Identifier typeJavaPython
ClassUse CamelCase for multi-word classesUse snake_case for multi-word classes
Functionuse verbs or verb phrasesuse lowercase_with_underscores
Procedureuse verbs or verb phrasesuse lowercase_with_underscores
VariablecamelCaselowercase_with_underscores
ConstantAll uppercase words separated by underscoresAll uppercase words separated by underscores
PackageLowercase words separated by dotsLowercase words separated by underscores
  • Java uses the toString() function to return objects' information.
  • Python can refer to attributes directly or use the __str()__ function to return objects' information

Data typesвҖӢ

Data TypeSizeDefault valueDescription
byte1 byte08-bit signed integer
short2 bytes016-bit signed integer
int4 bytes032-bit signed integer
long8 bytes064-bit signed integer
float4 bytes0.0f32-bit floating point
double8 bytes0.0d64-bit floating point
boolean1 bitfalsetrue or false
char2 bytes'\u0000'16-bit Unicode character

Non-Primitive Data TypesвҖӢ

  • Non-primitive: Arrays, Classes, Interfaces, and Strings.
  • Non-primitive data types are by default set to null in Java, None in Python.

VariablesвҖӢ

  • Static: enables the variable to be used without creating an object of its defining class.
  • Final: makes the variable unchangeable.

OperatorsвҖӢ

Operator CategoryJavaPython
Unaryexpr++ expr--
++expr --expr +expr -expr+expr -expr
Arithmetic* / &* / &
+ -+ -
Relational< > <= >=< > <= >=
== !=== !=
Logical! &&not and
||or
Ternary(expr1) ? <expr2> : <expr3>(expr1) if <expr2> then <expr3>
Assignment= += -= *= /= %== += -= *= /= %= **=
Identity/Membershipis is not in not in
  • Java: boolean q = (5 % 2 != 2) ? true : false
  • Python: q = True if (5 % 2 != 2) else False

Standard InputвҖӢ

import java.util.Scanner;

public class Inputs {
  static Scanner in = new Scanner(System.in);

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    System.out.print("X = ");
    int x = in.nextInt();
    System.out.println("x squared = " + Math.pow(x, 2));
  }
}
import sys

x = int(input("x = "))

print("x squared = ", pow(x, 2))

StringвҖӢ

String (java)вҖӢ

Immutable

  • String s1 = "Hello";: initialize using literal syntax
  • String s2 = new String("Hello");: initialize using a constructor
s1 == s1 // false
s1.equals(s2) // true

String Format (Python)вҖӢ

SymbolMeaningExample codeOutput
<Left alignf'[{42:<5}]'[42 ]
>Right alignf'[{42:>5}]'[ 42]
^Center alignf'[{42:^5}]'[ 42 ]
< with fill charLeft align with custom fillf'[{42:-<5}]'[42---]
> with fill charRight align with custom fillf'[{42:->5}]'[---42]
^ with fill charCenter align with custom fillf'[{42:->5}]'[-42--]

ArrayвҖӢ

Array (java)вҖӢ

int[] x = {2, 4, -1, 11, 3};

  • Declaration: int[] x
  • Instantiation: x = new int[5];
  • Initialization: x[0] = 2; x[1] = 4; x[2] = -1;

item.title: Introduction to ai @002
item.content:encoded:
EnvironmentвҖӢ

  • All possible state and information about how the states are related.
  • The costs from one state to each of its adjacent states are also given.

AgentвҖӢ

  • Simulated intelligence knows which state it is in.
  • If it takes an action at a given state, it knows the next state and the corresponding cost.

Characteristics of the environmentвҖӢ

  • Fully Observable: The agent always knows the current state of the environment at each point in time.
  • Deterministic: The next state of the environment is completely determined by the current state and the action taken by the agent.
  • Static: The environment is unchanged.
  • Discrete: A limited number of distinct, clearly defined actions.
  • Single agent: An agent operating by itself in an environment.

Search problemвҖӢ

Finding a path from a starting point to a goal point in a space.

  • The initial state
  • State space: The environment or area where the search takes place
  • A set of actions: The possible actions that the agent can take in each state.
    • ACTION (s)
  • A transition model:
    • takes in a state and an action.
    • returns the successor state, which is any state reachable from doing action a in state s.
    • RESULT(s, a)
  • A goal state:
    • The target location or position that needs to be reached.
    • represented by a goal test function
  • A path cost function:
    • The cost associated with a particular path taken through the state space.
    • c(s1, a, s2)

FrontierвҖӢ

  • A set of nodes that are under consideration to be expanded.
  • A set of leaf nodes in the search spanning tree are available for expansion at any given step.
  • A search algorithm determines how to choose a node in the Frontier to grow the search spanning tree.

Search AlgorithmвҖӢ

Tree Search vs Graph SearchвҖӢ

Explored Set

  • The frontier in graph search separates the search-space graph into two regions, the explored region and the unexplored region, so that Every path from the initial state to an unexplored state has to pass through a state in the frontier.

Performance measuresвҖӢ

  • Completeness
  • Cost Optimality
  • Time complexity
  • Space complexity

BFSвҖӢ

Queue

BFS TreeвҖӢ

from collections import deque

def bfs_tree(start, goal_test, successors):
    """
    start: мӢңмһ‘ мғҒнғң
    goal_test(s): лӘ©н‘ң кІҖмӮ¬ н•ЁмҲҳ -> bool
    successors(s): мғҒнғң sм—җм„ң к°Ҳ мҲҳ мһҲлҠ” лӢӨмқҢ мғҒнғңл“Өмқҳ лҰ¬мҠӨнҠё л°ҳнҷҳ

    л°ҳнҷҳ: лӘ©н‘ңм—җ лҸ„лӢ¬н•ҳлҠ” кІҪлЎң(list) лҳҗлҠ” None
    (Tree-search: explored/мӨ‘ліө мІҙнҒ¬ м•Ҳ н•Ё)
    """
    if goal_test(start):
        return [start]

    # л…ёл“ң = (state, parent_index)
    nodes = [(start, None)]
    frontier = deque([0])  # nodesмқҳ мқёлҚұмҠӨлҘј нҒҗм—җ м ҖмһҘ

    while frontier:
        parent_idx = frontier.popleft()
        parent_state, _ = nodes[parent_idx]

        for nxt in successors(parent_state):
            nodes.append((nxt, parent_idx))
            child_idx = len(nodes) - 1

            if goal_test(nxt):
                # кІҪлЎң ліөмӣҗ
                path, i = [], child_idx
                while i is not None:
                    path.append(nodes[i][0])
                    i = nodes[i][1]
                return list(reversed(path))

            frontier.append(child_idx)

    return None
from collections import deque

def bfs_graph(start, goal_test, successors):
    """
    start: мӢңмһ‘ мғҒнғң (мҳҲ: 'Arad')
    goal_test(s): sк°Җ лӘ©н‘ңл©ҙ True
    successors(s): мғҒнғң sм—җм„ң (лӢӨмқҢмғҒнғң, л№„мҡ©) нҳ№мқҖ к·ёлғҘ лӢӨмқҢмғҒнғң лҰ¬мҠӨнҠё л°ҳнҷҳ
                   м•„лһҳм—җм„ңлҠ” лӢӨмқҢмғҒнғң лҰ¬мҠӨнҠёлқјкі  к°Җм •
    л°ҳнҷҳ: start -> ... -> goal кІҪлЎң лҰ¬мҠӨнҠё, м—Ҷмңјл©ҙ None
    """
    # л…ёл“ң = (state, parent_index)
    frontier = deque([(start, None)])   # FIFO нҒҗ
    frontier_states = {start}           # frontierм—җ мһҲлҠ” мғҒнғң м§‘н•© (мӨ‘ліө л°©м§Җ)
    explored = set()                    # мқҙлҜё нҷ•мһҘн•ң мғҒнғң(Closed)

    # кІҪлЎң ліөмӣҗмқ„ мң„н•ҙ лӘЁл“  л…ёл“ңлҘј л°°м—ҙм—җ л”°лЎң м ҖмһҘ
    nodes = [(start, None)]             # nodes[i] = (state, parent_index)
    index_in_queue = deque([0])         # frontierм—җм„ңмқҳ мқёлҚұмҠӨ(=nodesмқҳ мқёлҚұмҠӨ)

    if goal_test(start):
        return [start]

    while frontier:
        state, parent = frontier.popleft()
        node_idx = index_in_queue.popleft()
        frontier_states.discard(state)
        explored.add(state)

        for nxt in successors(state):
            if (nxt not in explored) and (nxt not in frontier_states):
                # child л…ёл“ң м ҖмһҘ
                nodes.append((nxt, node_idx))
                child_idx = len(nodes) - 1

                if goal_test(nxt):
                    # кІҪлЎң ліөмӣҗ
                    path = []
                    i = child_idx
                    while i is not None:
                        path.append(nodes[i][0])
                        i = nodes[i][1]
                    return list(reversed(path))

                # frontierм—җ мӮҪмһ…
                frontier.append((nxt, node_idx))
                index_in_queue.append(child_idx)
                frontier_states.add(nxt)

    return None
graph = {
    "Arad": ["Sibiu", "Timisoara", "Zerind"],
    "Sibiu": ["Arad", "Fagaras"],
    "Timisoara": ["Arad", "Lugoj"],
    "Zerind": ["Arad"],
    "Fagaras": [],
    "Lugoj": []
}

path = bfs(
    start="Arad",
    goal_test=lambda s: s == "Lugoj",
    successors=lambda s: graph.get(s, [])
)
print(path)
  • Has the shallowest path to every node on the frontier
  • memory-intensive as it stores all nodes.

DFSвҖӢ

Stack

def depth_first_search(initial_state, goal_test, actions):
    """
    initial_state: мӢңмһ‘ мғҒнғң
    goal_test(s): мғҒнғң sк°Җ лӘ©н‘ңл©ҙ True
    actions(s): мғҒнғң sм—җм„ң мқҙлҸҷ к°ҖлҠҘн•ң лӢӨмқҢ мғҒнғңл“Өмқҳ лҰ¬мҠӨнҠё л°ҳнҷҳ
    л°ҳнҷҳ: start вҶ’ goal кІҪлЎң(list) лҳҗлҠ” None
    """

    # лӘЁл“  л…ёл“ң м ҖмһҘ: nodes[i] = (state, parent_index)
    nodes = [(initial_state, None)]

    # frontier вҶҗ FILO мҠӨнғқ (м—¬кё°м„ңлҠ” л…ёл“ң мқёлҚұмҠӨл§Ң м ҖмһҘ)
    frontier = [0]

    # frontierм—җ мһҲлҠ” мғҒнғңл“Өмқҳ м§‘н•© (мӨ‘ліө мӮҪмһ… л°©м§Җмҡ©)
    stacked_states = {initial_state}

    # explored вҶҗ мқҙлҜё нҷ•мһҘ(мһҗмӢқ мғқм„ұ)н•ң мғҒнғңл“Өмқҳ м§‘н•©
    explored = set()

    # мӢңмһ‘ мғҒнғңк°Җ лӘ©н‘ңлқјл©ҙ л°”лЎң л°ҳнҷҳ
    if goal_test(initial_state):
        return [initial_state]

    # DFS лЈЁн”„ мӢңмһ‘
    while True:
        # frontierк°Җ л№„л©ҙ мӢӨнҢЁ
        if not frontier:
            return None

        # мҠӨнғқм—җм„ң л§Ё мң„ л…ёл“ң кәјлӮҙкё°
        node_idx = frontier.pop()
        state, parent_idx = nodes[node_idx]

        # мҠӨнғқ мғҒнғң м§‘н•©м—җм„ң м ңкұ° (мқҙм ң нҷ•мһҘн•  м°ЁлЎҖ)
        stacked_states.discard(state)

        # нҳ„мһ¬ мғҒнғңм—җм„ң к°ҖлҠҘн•ң лӘЁл“  мһҗмӢқ мғҒнғң нҷ•мқё
        for child_state in actions(state):
            # мһҗмӢқ мғҒнғңк°Җ exploredлӮҳ frontierм—җ м—Ҷмқ„ л•Ңл§Ң мІҳлҰ¬
            if (child_state not in explored) and (child_state not in stacked_states):
                # мғҲ л…ёл“ң м ҖмһҘ (л¶ҖлӘЁлҠ” нҳ„мһ¬ л…ёл“ң)
                nodes.append((child_state, node_idx))
                child_idx = len(nodes) - 1

                # лӘ©н‘ң мғҒнғңл©ҙ кІҪлЎң ліөмӣҗн•ҙм„ң л°ҳнҷҳ
                if goal_test(child_state):
                    path, i = [], child_idx
                    while i is not None:
                        path.append(nodes[i][0])
                        i = nodes[i][1]
                    return list(reversed(path))

                # лӘ©н‘ңк°Җ м•„лӢҲл©ҙ мҠӨнғқм—җ push
                frontier.append(child_idx)
                stacked_states.add(child_state)

        # лӘЁл“  мһҗмӢқ мІҳлҰ¬к°Җ лҒқлӮҳл©ҙ exploredм—җ м¶”к°Җ
        explored.add(state)
  • Low memory usage
  • Can get stuck in deep or infinite branches (Not cost-optimal)

UCSвҖӢ

Priority Queue

  • lowest path cost f(n) = g(n)
  • Best-first search with the evaluation function
  • Uniform-cost search is complete and cost optimal
  • Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from the root node to every other node in a graph with non-negative edge weights.
  • A special case of Dijkstra's algorithm in which the
import heapq

def uniform_cost_search(initial_state, goal_test, actions, step_cost):
    """
    initial_state: мӢңмһ‘ мғҒнғң
    goal_test(s): мғҒнғң sк°Җ лӘ©н‘ңл©ҙ True
    actions(s): мғҒнғң sм—җм„ң к°ҖлҠҘн•ң лӢӨмқҢ мғҒнғң лҰ¬мҠӨнҠё
    step_cost(s, s_next): s -> s_next мқҙлҸҷ л№„мҡ© (м–‘мҲҳ к°Җм •)

    л°ҳнҷҳ: start вҶ’ goal кІҪлЎң(list) лҳҗлҠ” None
    """

    # лӘЁл“  л…ёл“ң м ҖмһҘ: nodes[i] = (state, parent_idx, path_cost)
    nodes = [(initial_state, None, 0.0)]

    # frontier вҶҗ PATH-COST кё°мӨҖ мөңмҶҢ нһҷ (мӣҗмҶҢ: (cost, node_idx))
    frontier = [(0.0, 0)]
    heapq.heapify(frontier)

    # frontierм—җ мһҲлҠ” мғҒнғңмқҳ нҳ„мһ¬ мөңм Җ л№„мҡ©(л©ӨлІ„мӢӯ/л№„мҡ© л№„көҗмҡ©)
    frontier_costs = {initial_state: 0.0}

    # explored вҶҗ мқҙлҜё нҷ•мһҘ мҷ„лЈҢн•ң мғҒнғң м§‘н•©
    explored = set()

    # мӢңмһ‘мқҙ кі§ лӘ©н‘ңл©ҙ л°”лЎң л°ҳнҷҳ
    if goal_test(initial_state):
        return [initial_state]

    # loop do
    while frontier:
        # node вҶҗ POP(frontier)  /* мөңмҶҢ л№„мҡ© л…ёл“ң */
        cost, node_idx = heapq.heappop(frontier)
        state, parent_idx, path_cost = nodes[node_idx]

        # нһҷм—җ лӮЁм•„ мһҲлҠ” кө¬лІ„м „(лҚ” л№„мӢј лІ„м „)мқҙл©ҙ кұҙл„ҲлӣҙлӢӨ
        if state in frontier_costs and cost != frontier_costs[state]:
            continue

        # goal test (мҠҲлҸ„мҪ”л“ң: pop м§Ғнӣ„ кІҖмӮ¬)
        if goal_test(state):
            # SOLUTION(node) вҶ’ кІҪлЎң ліөмӣҗ
            path = []
            i = node_idx
            while i is not None:
                path.append(nodes[i][0])
                i = nodes[i][1]
            return list(reversed(path))

        # add node.STATE to explored
        explored.add(state)
        # frontier лӘ©лЎқм—җм„ң мқҙ мғҒнғң м ңкұ°(лҚ” мқҙмғҒ frontierм—җ м—ҶмқҢ)
        frontier_costs.pop(state, None)

        # for each action in ACTIONS(node.STATE) do
        for nxt in actions(state):
            new_cost = path_cost + step_cost(state, nxt)

            # child.STATE not in explored or frontier ?
            in_explored = (nxt in explored)
            in_frontier = (nxt in frontier_costs)

            # (1) explored/ fronter м–ҙл””м—җлҸ„ м—Ҷмңјл©ҙ мғҲлЎң мӮҪмһ…
            if not in_explored and not in_frontier:
                nodes.append((nxt, node_idx, new_cost))
                child_idx = len(nodes) - 1
                heapq.heappush(frontier, (new_cost, child_idx))
                frontier_costs[nxt] = new_cost

            # (2) frontierм—җ мһҲлҠ”лҚ°, лҚ” мӢј кІҪлЎңлҘј м°ҫм•ҳлӢӨл©ҙ "көҗмІҙ"
            elif in_frontier and new_cost < frontier_costs[nxt]:
                nodes.append((nxt, node_idx, new_cost))
                child_idx = len(nodes) - 1
                heapq.heappush(frontier, (new_cost, child_idx))
                # нҳ„мһ¬ мөңм Җл№„мҡ©мқ„ к°ұмӢ  вҶ’ мқҙм „ нһҷ н•ӯлӘ©мқҖ лӮҳмӨ‘м—җ нҢқлҗ  л•Ң л№„мҡ©л¶Ҳмқјм№ҳлЎң мһҗлҸҷ л¬ҙмӢң
                frontier_costs[nxt] = new_cost

    # if EMPTY?(frontier) then failure
    return None

Greedy Best First SearchвҖӢ

  • f(n) = h(n)
  • h(n)=hSLDh(n) = h_{SLD}h(n)=hSLDвҖӢ, where SLDSLDSLD for the Straight-Line Distance
  • It expands the node with the lowest h(n)h(n)h(n) value at each step
from heapq import heappush, heappop

def gbfs_path(G, start, goal, heuristic):
    """
    Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS)
    G: мқём ‘ лҰ¬мҠӨнҠё dict, G[u] = мқҙмӣғл“Өмқҳ лҰ¬мҠӨнҠё/мқҙн„°лҹ¬лё”
    heuristic(x, goal): м¶”м •кұ°лҰ¬ h(x)
    л°ҳнҷҳ: start -> ... -> goal кІҪлЎң(list) лҳҗлҠ” None
    """

    # мҡ°м„ мҲңмң„ нҒҗ мӣҗмҶҢ: (h(state), state, path)
    pq = []
    heappush(pq, (heuristic(start, goal), start, [start]))

    visited = set()          # мқҙлҜё кәјлӮҙм„ң нҷ•мһҘн•ң л…ёл“ң(мһ¬л°©л¬ё л°©м§Җ)
    in_frontier = {start}    # нҒҗм—җ л“Өм–ҙк°„ л…ёл“ң(мӨ‘ліө мӮҪмһ… л°©м§Җ)

    while pq:
        # нңҙлҰ¬мҠӨнӢұмқҙ к°ҖмһҘ мһ‘мқҖ л…ёл“ңлҘј кәјлғ„
        _, vertex, path = heappop(pq)
        in_frontier.discard(vertex)

        # мқҙлҜё нҷ•мһҘн–ҲлӢӨл©ҙ мҠӨнӮө
        if vertex in visited:
            continue
        visited.add(vertex)

        # лӘ©н‘ңл©ҙ кІҪлЎң л°ҳнҷҳ
        if vertex == goal:
            return path

        # мқҙмӣғмқ„ нңҙлҰ¬мҠӨнӢұ мҲңмңјлЎң нҒҗм—җ м¶”к°Җ
        for neighbor in G.get(vertex, []):
            if neighbor in visited or neighbor in in_frontier:
                continue
            heappush(pq, (heuristic(neighbor, goal), neighbor, path + [neighbor]))
            in_frontier.add(neighbor)

    return None

A* SearchвҖӢ

  • f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
  • The most common informed search algorithm.
  • The tree-search version of A* is optimal if h(n) is an admissible heuristic.
  • The graph-search version is optimal if h(n) is consistent.
def astar_path(G, start, goal):
    """
    Find a path from start to goal using A* Search.
    G: NetworkX Graph
    start: мӢңмһ‘ л…ёл“ң
    goal: лӘ©н‘ң л…ёл“ң
    """
    pq = PriorityQueue()
    # мӢңмһ‘ л…ёл“ңлҘј кІҪлЎң лҰ¬мҠӨнҠёмҷҖ н•Ёк»ҳ нҒҗм—җ м¶”к°Җ, f = 0
    pq.push((start, [start]), 0)
    visited = set()

    while pq:
        (vertex, path) = pq.pop()

        # мқҙлҜё л°©л¬ён–ҲлӢӨл©ҙ мҠӨнӮө
        if vertex in visited:
            continue
        visited.add(vertex)

        # лӘ©н‘ң лҸ„м°© мӢң кІҪлЎң л°ҳнҷҳ
        if vertex == goal:
            return path

        # мқём ‘ л…ёл“ң нғҗмғү
        for neighbor in G[vertex]:
            if neighbor in visited:
                continue
            # g(n) = нҳ„мһ¬ кІҪлЎңк№Ңм§Җмқҳ мӢӨм ң л№„мҡ©
            g_cost = nx.path_weight(G, path + [neighbor], 'weight')
            # h(n) = нңҙлҰ¬мҠӨнӢұ(лӘ©н‘ңк№Ңм§Җмқҳ м¶”м • л№„мҡ©)
            h_cost = heuristic(cities[neighbor], cities[goal])
            f_cost = g_cost + h_cost

            pq.push((neighbor, path + [neighbor]), f_cost)

    return None

AdmissibilityвҖӢ

  • h(n)вүӨhвҲ—(n)h(n) \leq h^*(n)h(n)вүӨhвҲ—(n)
  • Never overestimate the cost to reach the goal
  • A straight line distance between a node and the goal node is an admissible heuristic as it is always shorter than the actual distance between this node to the goal node.
  • With an admissible heuristic, A* is cost-optimal.

ConsistencyвҖӢ

  • h(n)вүӨc(n,a,nвҖІ)+h(nвҖІ)h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')h(n)вүӨc(n,a,nвҖІ)+h(nвҖІ)
  • h(n) is consistent if the estimated cost is always less than or equal to the actual cost.

Admissible vs ConsistentвҖӢ

  • Consistent вҮ’ Admissible (лӘЁл“  consistent нңҙлҰ¬мҠӨнӢұмқҖ admissible)
  • Admissible вҮҸ Consistent (кұ°кҫёлЎңлҠ” м„ұлҰҪ м•Ҳ н•Ё)
  • The tree search version of A* is optimal if h(n) is admissible
  • The graph search version of A* is optimal if h(n) is consistent

SummaryвҖӢ

Measure / CriteriaBFSDFSUniform CostA*
Complete?YesNoYesYes
Time complexityO(bd)O(b^d)O(bd)O(bm)O(b^m)O(bm)O(b1+вҢҠCвҲ—/ПөвҢӢ)O\left(b^{1 + \lfloor C^* / \epsilon \rfloor}\right)O(b1+вҢҠCвҲ—/ПөвҢӢ)O(bd)O(b^d)O(bd)
Space complexityO(bd)O(b^d)O(bd)O(bm)O(bm)O(bm)O(b1+вҢҠCвҲ—/ПөвҢӢ)O\left(b^{1 + \lfloor C^* / \epsilon \rfloor}\right)O(b1+вҢҠCвҲ—/ПөвҢӢ)O(bd)O(b^d)O(bd)
Cost optimal?YesNoYesYes
  • Пө\epsilonПө is the smallest positive cost of any single step (edge) in the search problem.

item.title: Fundamentals of Data Analytics @002
item.content:encoded:
Business IntelligenceвҖӢ

KDDвҖӢ

knowledge discovery in databases (KDD) refers to the comprehensive process of finding knowledge in data.

  • Learning from the application domain
  • Creating a target dataset
  • Data cleansing/pre-processing
  • Data reduction/projection
  • Choosing the function of data mining
  • Choosing the data mining algorithm
  • Data mining
  • Interpretation
  • Using discovered knowledge

CRISP-DMвҖӢ

The Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM) methodology provides a structured approach to planning a data mining project. As it is a cross-industry standard, it is widely used by practitioners who need a repeatable approach for data mining projects and can be used in a variety of machine learning projects.

  • Business understanding: Set up a business problem and understand what you want to accomplish from a business perspective.
  • Data understanding: Identify, collect and review the required data.
  • Data preparation: Prepare your data for modeling.
  • Modeling: Analyze possible approaches and develop the model.
  • Evaluation: Evaluate results against business needs.
  • Deployment: Deploy the model.

Statistical AnalysisвҖӢ

Data analytics has borrowed from statistical analysis, which involves collecting data, counting, probabilities, and hypothesis testing.

The two main approaches that are relevant to data analytics are:

Descriptive StatisticsвҖӢ

  • Purpose: Analyze past events using historical data
  • Data Source: Stored data from previous activities
  • Application: Assists companies to make informed decisions based on statistical analysis of historical patterns
  • Focus: "What happened?" - Understanding past performance and trends

Predictive StatisticsвҖӢ

  • Purpose: Predict future events based on currently available data
  • Data Source: Present and historical data combined with analytical models
  • Application: Provides statements or predictions about events that have not yet occurred
  • Focus: "What will happen?" - Forecasting future outcomes and behaviors

Data analytics resultsвҖӢ

The presentation of data analytics results needs to be understandable by humans, easily used, and accurate on computers.

The effectiveness of different data analytics methods can be evaluated across two dimensions:

  • X-axis: Computer accuracy (how accurate the method is)
  • Y-axis: Human understandability (how easily humans can interpret the results)

Ethical PrinciplesвҖӢ

Despite the proliferation of ethical principles, some are especially significant for data analytics and AI solutions and must be implemented as mandatory ethical principles.

The five core mandatory ethical principles for AI and data analytics are:

1. TransparencyвҖӢ

The need to describe, inspect and reproduce the mechanisms through which AI systems make decisions and learn to adapt to their environment.

Key Components (EU AI HLEG):

  • Traceability: Understanding the data flow and decision path
  • Explainability: Providing reasonable explanations for AI outputs
  • Communication: Clear information sharing with stakeholders

Stakeholder Requirements:

  • Users: Understanding what the system is doing and why
  • Creators: Validation and certification of AI systems
  • Operators: Understanding processes and input data
  • Investigators: Accident investigation capabilities
  • Regulators: Investigation and compliance support
  • Legal System: Evidence and decision-making support
  • Public: Building confidence in technology

2. FairnessвҖӢ

A complex, multi-faceted concept ensuring AI systems do not discriminate against individuals or groups.

Types of Fairness:

  • Process Fairness: Ethical methods regardless of outcome
  • Outcome Fairness: Ensuring algorithmic outputs don't perpetuate bias

Ethical Perspectives:

  • Equity: Discretion and fairness in applying justice
  • Social Justice: Equality and solidarity in society
  • Distributive Justice: Appropriate distribution of benefits
  • Procedural Justice: Fair allocation procedures
  • Interactional Justice: Appropriate interpersonal treatment

EU AI HLEG Components:

  • Avoidance of bias
  • Accessibility and universal design
  • Stakeholder participation

3. AccountabilityвҖӢ

Clear acknowledgement and assumption of responsibility for AI actions, decisions, and impacts.

Three Types of AI Accountability:

  1. System-Level: AI's ability to explain and justify decisions
  2. Individual/Group: Determining who is responsible for AI impacts
  3. Sociotechnical: Broader system accountability for development and deployment

EU AI HLEG Components:

  • Auditability: Systems can be examined and verified
  • Impact Reporting: Minimizing and documenting negative effects
  • Trade-off Documentation: Recording decision rationales
  • Redress Ability: Mechanisms for addressing harm

4. PrivacyвҖӢ

The right to control how personal data is collected, stored, modified, used, and exchanged.

Seven Types of Privacy (Finn et al.):

  1. Privacy of the Person: Body functions and characteristics (biometrics, genetics)
  2. Privacy of Behaviour: Sensitive activities (political, religious, sexual preferences)
  3. Privacy of Communication: Private communications protection
  4. Privacy of Data and Image: Control over personal data and images
  5. Privacy of Thoughts and Feelings: Mental privacy rights
  6. Privacy of Location and Space: Movement without tracking
  7. Privacy of Association: Freedom to associate without monitoring

Key Considerations:

  • GDPR Compliance: EU data protection regulations
  • Data Minimization: Using only necessary data
  • Consent Management: Clear user permissions
  • Data Security: Protecting against breaches

EU AI HLEG Components:

  • Respect for privacy and data protection
  • Quality and integrity of data
  • Access to data

5. Community BenefitвҖӢ

AI should deliver clear community or government benefits and maximize social value.

Core Requirements:

  • Public Good: Solutions must serve broader community interests
  • Benefit Maximization: Optimizing positive social impact
  • Alternative Consideration: Evaluating AI against other analysis tools
  • Default Principle: Should be standard for all AI solutions

KNIMEвҖӢ

item.title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence @001
item.content:encoded:
AIвҖӢ

AI refers to the simulation of human intelligence in machines that are programmed to think like humans and mimic their actions.

  • As a research field, AI aims to develop techniques including algorithms, methods and models to enable systems to perform tasks which require intelligence when performed by humans.
  • AI is concerned with developing machines or computer agents that are capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence.
  • AI is about the study and construction of agent programs that perform well in a given environment, for a given agent architecture.
  • AI is a true universal field.
  • The term "Artificial Intelligence" was coined by John McCarthy for the Dartmouth Summer Research Project in 1956, marking the formal beginning of AI as a research field.

HistoryвҖӢ

  • 1943-1956: Inception of AI
  • 1966-1973: A dose of reality
  • 1969-1986: Expert systems
  • 2011-present: Deep learning

Turing reward winnersвҖӢ

  • Defining the foundation of the field based on representation and reasoning
    • 1969 Marvin Minsky
    • 1971 John McCarthy
  • Making fundamental contributions to AI and human cognition
    • 1975 Allen Newell & Herbert Simon
  • Developing expert systems that encode human knowledge to solve real-world problems
    • Ed Feigenbaum & Raj Reddy
  • Honored for "probably approximately correct learning (PAC learning)", a foundational theoretical framework for AI and ML
    • 2010 Leslie Valiant
  • Developing probabilistic reasoning techniques that deal with uncertainty in a principled manner
    • 2011 Judea Pearl
  • Making "deep learning" a critical part of modern computing.
    • 2018 Yoshua Bengio & Geoffrey Hinton & Yann LeCun
  • Recognized for lifetime contributions to reinforcement learning, a core method in modern AI
    • 2024 Andrew Barto & Richard Sutton

Nobel Prize in PhysicsвҖӢ

  • For foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks
    • 2024 John J. Hopfield & Geoffrey Hinton

Dimensions of AIвҖӢ

Action (Behavior) vs Thinking (Thought)

AI TreeвҖӢ

PEASвҖӢ

  • Performance: The performance measure that defines the criterion of success
  • Environment: The agentвҖҷs prior knowledge of the environment
  • Actuators: The actions that the agent can perform through actuators
  • Sensors: The agentвҖҷs percept sequence to date through sensors
Agent TypePerformance MeasureEnvironmentActuatorsSensors
Taxi driverSafe, fast, comfortable transportationRoads, traffic, passengersSteering wheel, accelerator, brakesCameras, GPS, speedometer
Medical diagnosis systemHealthy patient, reduced costsPatient, hospital, staffDisplay of questions, tests, diagnosis, treatmentsTouchscreen/voice entry of symptoms and findings
Satellite image analysis systemCorrect categorization of objects, terrainOrbiting satellite, downlink weatherDisplay of scene categorizationHigh-resolution digital camera

Rational AgentвҖӢ

Agent = architecture + program

  • Agent function: abstract mathematical description
  • Agent program: concrete implementation, running within some physical system
  • Agent architecture: running on some sort of computing device with physical sensors and actuators

Weak vs Strong AIвҖӢ

  • Weak AI: treats a computer as a device that can simulate the performance of a brain
  • Strong AI: a theoretical form of machine intelligence that is equal to human intelligence.

Why AI becoming so hotвҖӢ

  • Big data
  • Cloud computing
  • Deep learning

RisksвҖӢ

  • Lethal autonomous weapons
  • Surveillance and persuasion
  • Biased decision making

item.title: Fundamentals of software development @002
item.content:encoded:
Classes & ObjectsвҖӢ

ClassвҖӢ

  • A template that defines the attributes and methods of an object, which can be used to create many objects.
  • Name of the class is noun.
  • Functions are defined in the class, containing the function data.
  • Many objects can be created from the same class.
  • A class can be inherited by many sub-classes
  • A child class can have one or more parent classes. (super-classes)
  • Encapsulated data
  • Behaviors of a class are exposed while the implementation is hidden.

ObjectвҖӢ

  • An instance of a class, created the class template during runtime.
  • Object-oriented software is composed of many objects
  • a composite data type identified by its attributes (fields) and behaviors (functions), which are defined in the class.
  • object-oriented design analysis: to identify the classes and relationships between the classes to model how the system would work.

Procedure & FunctionвҖӢ

AspectProcedureFunction
Returns value?вқҢ Usually does not return a valueвң… Usually returns a value
PurposePerform an actionCompute and return a result

Object-Oriented Paradigm PrinciplesвҖӢ

AbstractionвҖӢ

  • hides the internal implementation of functions and expose to other class the behavior of those functions.
  • Data abstraction: hide the variables stored the data and only expose the values.
  • Function abstraction: hide the function code and only export the function behavior.

EncapsulationвҖӢ

  • a fundamental principle of OOP, increases data and code security, enhances performance by reducing object interactions.
  • minimize the number of interactions from outside.

InheritanceвҖӢ

  • allows classes to have sub-classes.
  • a parent class can have many children.
  • a child class can have more than one parent.
  • a child class inherits their parent class and can also override them.

PolymorphismвҖӢ

  • more of the same type of object can be used interchangeably.
  • object of different types to be treated as objects of a common parent.
  • multiple object types with the same parent can implement the same function in different ways.
  • multiple object types with the same parent can use the same attributes with different values.

Object-Oriented DesignвҖӢ

  • The process of designing a software using oop.
  • Identify the classes -> Identify the classes' attributes and behaviors -> Identify the how classes interact with each other and create a visual class model

Identify classesвҖӢ

  • Classes are derived from use cases and actors described in the requirement analysis process.
  • Classes should be identified exactly as needed.
  • Reduce coupling and enable extensibility.
  • Identifying a class always requires to identify the fields and methods.
  • A class can use the methods of another class. -> one class (the client) uses or depends on another class (the supplier).

UML Class diagramвҖӢ

  • UML visual representation of the object-oriented software system.
  • Describes the attributes and operations of classes, relationships, and any constraints.
  • Used as a plan to construct executable code and define the way in which objects may interact.

AssociationsвҖӢ

  • a relationship between classes indicating a meaningful connection.
  • Labelled by
    • Association name
    • Role name
    • Multiplicity
    • Navigability

MultiplicityвҖӢ

  • how many instances of type A can be associated with on instance of type B.

Recursive or reflexive associationвҖӢ

  • a class can have an association with itself.

AttributesвҖӢ

  • m:m association can be divided into two 1:m associations.

Association ClassesвҖӢ

  • An attribute is related to an association.
  • Instance of the association class have a life-time dependency on the association.
  • an associative type should exist in the background somewhere.

Generalization & SpecializationвҖӢ

  • Generalization: the activity of identifying commonalities among concepts and defining superclass (general concept) and subclass (specialized concepts) relationships.
  • "is-a" relationship
  • a subclass inherits from a superclass.

Benefits of generalizationвҖӢ

  • Code Reusability
  • Cleaner/Simplified design
  • Scalability
  • Extensibility
  • Easier Maintenance
  • Testability
  • Modularity

Class inheritanceвҖӢ

  • same as generalization and specialization.
  • the subclasses inherit the attributes and methods of the superclasses.
  • When a hierarchy is created, statements about the superclass apply to subclasses.
  • A conceptual subclass should be a member of the set of the superclass.
  • The conceptual subclass is a kind of superclass.
  • <subclass> is a <superclass>
  • Every instance of the <subclass> can be viewed as an instance of the <superclass>.

SubclassвҖӢ

  • has additional attributes of interest.
  • has additional associations of interest.
  • is handled differently than the superclass or other subclasses.
  • represents an object that behaves differently than the superclass or other subclasses.

SuperclassвҖӢ

  • Create a conceptual superclass when:
    • The potential conceptual subclasses represent variations of a similar concept.
    • The subclass fully conforms to the attributes and associations of its superclass. (100% rule)
  • All subclasses have the same attributes and operations which can be factored out and expressed in the superclass.
  • All subclasses have the same associations which can be factored out and related to the superclass.

Abstract ClassвҖӢ

  • a parent class that cannot be instantiated.
  • at least one of its operations is abstract.
  • an abstract operation has its signature defined in the abstract parent class, but the implementation is defined in the child class.
  • create a high-level modelling vocabulary.

Interface & RealizationвҖӢ

  • a class with no attributes.
  • can't be instantiated.
  • simply declares a contract that may be realized by zero or more classes.
  • to separate the specification from its implementation.
  • only defines a specification for what the class should do and it never implies how it should do it.
  • the class implementing an interface has a realization relationship with the interface.

AggregationвҖӢ

  • a type of whole-part relationship in which the aggregate is made up of many parts.
  • Signified with a hollow diamond.
  • implies the part may be in many composite instances.

CompositionвҖӢ

  • a stronger form of aggregation.
  • the multiplicity at the composite end may be at most one
  • Signified with a filled diamond.
  • There is a create-delete dependency. Their lifetime is bound within the lifetime of the composite.

DependencyвҖӢ

  • a relationship between two or more model elements whereby a change to one element
  • The most common dependency stereotype is <<use>>, which simply states that the client makes use of the supplier in some way.

VisibilityвҖӢ

  • + public
  • - private
  • # protected
  • ~ package

ExampleвҖӢ

UML ToolsвҖӢ

  • Draw.io
  • Lucidchart
  • Visual Paradigm
  • StarUML
  • Creately
  • PlantUML

item.title: л…јкұ° мһ‘м„ұ, Integrating Evidence in Writing
item.content:encoded:
л…јкұ° мһ‘м„ұ мӨ‘мҡ”м„ұвҖӢ

  • лӮҙ мЈјмһҘмқҖ л¬ён—ҢВ·м—°кө¬В·м „л¬ёк°Җмқҳ к·јкұ° мң„м—җ м„ёмӣҢм ём•ј н•Ё
  • вҖҳлӮҳл§Ңмқҳ мқҳкІ¬вҖҷмқҙ м•„лӢҲлқј evidence-based writingмқҙ н•„мҲҳ
  • лҰ¬м„ңм№ҳ мҠӨнӮ¬В·л№„нҢҗм Ғ мӮ¬кі В·м—°кІ° лҠҘл Ҙмқ„ нҸүк°Җ

л…јкұ° нҶөн•© мҳҲмӢңвҖӢ

In order to transform ourselves, we need to transform our assumptions. Through this project, all stakeholders were forced to confront their mental models (assumptions about self, others and вҖңhow the world worksвҖқ вҖ“ see Senge, 1995 and Kim,1993) about psychiatric patients and psychiatric disorders. Through exposure to such patients on a regular basis, all came to question their tacit fears and prejudices with respect to this phenomenon and, through collective engagement with itвҖқ (2007,p.200).

  1. нҠ№м • мӣҗм№ҷ(leadership principle) лӘ…нҷ•нһҲ м ңмӢң
  2. ліёмқё м—°кө¬/мӮ¬лЎҖмҷҖ м—°кІ°
  3. кҙҖл Ё л…јл¬ё мқёмҡ©
  4. мӢӨм ң мӮ¬лЎҖВ·н”„лЎңм қнҠё кІ°кіјлЎң нҷ•мһҘ

л…јкұ° нҷңмҡ©лІ•вҖӢ

  • м§Ғм ‘ мқёмҡ©(quotation): мӣҗл¬ё к·ёлҢҖлЎң, л”°мҳҙн‘ң+м¶ңмІҳ (м „мІҙ кіјм ңмқҳ 10% мқҙн•ҳ к¶ҢмһҘ)
  • нҢЁлҹ¬н”„л ҲмқҙмҰҲ(paraphrase): м•„мқҙл””м–ҙлҘј лӮҙ л§җлЎң л°”кҝ” н‘ңнҳ„
  • мҡ”м•Ҫ(summarize): н•өмӢ¬л§Ң к°„кІ°н•ҳкІҢ м •лҰ¬

мқёмҡ© кө¬мЎ°вҖӢ

Author-prominentвҖӢ

м ҖмһҗмӨ‘мӢ¬

  • Author/year upfront: Goldsmith (2013, p.22) argues that as writing is not only a necessary attribute for engineering practice, but also a key element of learning and a means of clarifying oneвҖҷs thinking and of exploring ideas, its development needs to be both acknowledged and made visible in the engineering curriculum.
  • Author/year sandwich: Goldsmith identifies the lack of visibility of writing in the engineering curriculum as a key concern for engineering educators (2013, p.24).

Information-prominentвҖӢ

м •ліҙмӨ‘мӢ¬

  • The current delivery of the engineering curriculum in the majority of Australian universities reflects a positivist epistemology (Radcliffe, 2006).

Claim вҖ“ Evidence вҖ“ Comment кө¬мЎ°вҖӢ

  • Claim
    • There is abundant evidence and research that points to how much writing engineers, both practising engineers and engineering academics, actually do as part of their work.
  • Including evidence
    • Winsor (1990) makes the point that writing is viewed as part of an engineerвҖҷs job, but not part of engineering (p.58). However, despite the perception that engineers have of themselves as people who work with objects, вҖңwriting is what engineers doвҖқ (p.68).
  • Comment
    • This point illustrates an essential conflict in how engineers see their work and may explain why writing is not seen as integral to the engineering curriculum.
  • к·јкұ°(л¬ён—Ң/лҚ°мқҙн„°) мқёмҡ© нӣ„ лӮҙ н•ҙм„Ө(мҪ”л©ҳнҠё) л°ҳл“ңмӢң м¶”к°Җ
  • лӢЁмҲң мқёмҡ©/мҡ”м•Ҫ/нҢЁлҹ¬н”„л ҲмқҙмҰҲл§Ң м“°л©ҙ к°җм җ, лӮҙ л…јм§ҖмҷҖмқҳ м—°кІ°мқҙ н•„мҲҳ

Acknowledging verbsвҖӢ

м•„лһҳ лҸҷмӮ¬н‘ңлҘј нҷңмҡ©н•ҙм„ң, вҖҳмӮ¬мӢӨ лӢЁмҲң м „лӢ¬вҖҷ вҖҳмЈјмһҘ/к°•мЎ°вҖҷ вҖҳнҸүк°Җ/л№„нҢҗвҖҷ лүҳм•ҷмҠӨ н‘ңнҳ„

ліҙкі В·л¬ҳмӮ¬мЈјмһҘВ·к°•мЎ°нҸүк°ҖВ·лҸҷмқҳл№„нҢҗВ·л°ҳл°•
statesassertsagreesdisagrees
points outclaimsjustifiescontests
notesarguesaffirmsdisputes
remarkscontendsclarifiesrejects
describesmaintainssupportscounters
believesdemonstratesfinds/foundchallenges
addssuggestsconfirmselaborates
reportsimpliesindicatesillustrates
questionstheorizesputs forwardcounters
clarifiesoffersreasons
  • лҸҷмӮ¬ м„ нғқм—җ л”°лқј м •ліҙк°Җ мӮ¬мӢӨмқём§Җ, мЈјмһҘ/мқҳкІ¬/мқҙлЎ /л№„нҢҗмқём§Җ мқҳлҜёк°Җ лӢ¬лқјм§җ
  • лӢӨм–‘н•ң acknowledging verbsлҘј нҷңмҡ©н•ҙ л…јкұ°мҷҖ л№„нҢҗм Ғ мӮ¬кі л Ҙмқ„ л“ңлҹ¬лӮҙмһҗ

кІ°лЎ вҖӢ

лӮҙ мЈјмһҘмқҖ л¬ён—ҢВ·лҚ°мқҙн„°В·м „л¬ёк°Җ к·јкұ°м—җ м„ёмҡ°кі , к·јкұ° мқёмҡ© л’Өм—җлҠ” л°ҳл“ңмӢң лӮҙ мғқк°Ғ/мҪ”л©ҳнҠёлЎң л§Ҳл¬ҙлҰ¬

  • claim(мЈјмһҘ)-evidence(к·јкұ°)-comment(мҪ”л©ҳнҠё) кө¬мЎ°лҘј н•ӯмғҒ мқҳмӢқ
  • мқёмҡ©/нҢЁлҹ¬н”„л ҲмқҙмҰҲ/мҡ”м•Ҫ л°©мӢқ нҳјн•©
  • мқёмҡ©мқҖ л°ҳл“ңмӢң Harvard мҠӨнғҖмқјлЎң
  • мқёмҡ© л’Ө н•ҙм„Ө/м—°кІ°л¬ё(мҪ”л©ҳнҠё) л°ҳл“ңмӢң нҸ¬н•Ё

item.title: л ҲнҚјлҹ°мӢұ к°Җмқҙл“ң, Harvard referencing guide
item.content:encoded:
ліёл¬ё мқёмҡ©(In-text citation)вҖӢ

  • н•ң лӘ… м Җмһҗ
    • (Kim 2021)
  • л‘җ лӘ… м Җмһҗ
    • (Kim & Lee 2020)
  • 3лӘ… мқҙмғҒ
    • (Kim et al. 2018)
  • м§Ғм ‘ мқёмҡ©(нҺҳмқҙм§Җ)
    • (Smith 2009, p. 7)
  • м—ҶмқҢ/кё°кҙҖ/лҜёмғҒ
    • (Australian Bureau of Statistics 2009), (Brown n.d.)

м°ёкі л¬ён—Ң(Reference List) кё°ліё к·ңм№ҷвҖӢ

м Җмһҗ м„ұ, мқҙлӢҲм…ң. (м—°лҸ„). м ңлӘ©(мқҙнғӨлҰӯ), нҢҗ(н•„мҡ”мӢң). м¶ңнҢҗмӮ¬, м¶ңнҢҗм§Җ.

  • м ңлӘ©/м Җл„җлӘ…мқҖ мқҙнғӨлҰӯ
  • Reference ListлҠ” м•ҢнҢҢлІі мҲң
  • ліёл¬ёкіј Reference Listк°Җ м •нҷ•нһҲ мқјм№ҳн•ҙм•ј н•Ё
  • лӘЁл“  н•ӯлӘ©мқҖ л§Ҳм№Ён‘ңлЎң лҒқлӮЁ
  • м Җмһҗ 4лӘ… мқҙмғҒ: ліёл¬ём—” мІ« м Җмһҗ et al., Reference Listм—” лӘЁл‘җ мһ‘м„ұ

мһҗлЈҢмң нҳ•лі„ м°ёкі л¬ён—Ң мҳҲмӢңвҖӢ

мһҗлЈҢмң нҳ•мҳҲмӢң
лӢЁн–үліё(Book)Smith, J. 2022, Introduction to AI, Tech Press, Sydney.
н•ҷмҲ м§Җ л…јл¬ё(Journal Article)Kim, S. & Lee, J. 2020, вҖҳDeep learning for image recognitionвҖҷ, Journal of AI Research, vol. 15, no. 3, pp. 123-39.
мұ… мұ•н„°(Book Chapter)Brown, L. 2018, вҖҳData analysis techniquesвҖҷ, in S. Miller (ed.), Handbook of statistics, Data Publishing, Melbourne, pp. 45-67.
мӣ№мӮ¬мқҙнҠё(Website)UTS 2024, UTS Harvard referencing guide, viewed 31 July 2025, <https://www.lib.uts.edu.au/help/referencing/harvard-uts-referencing-guide>
ліҙкі м„ң/м •л¶Җ ліҙкі м„ң(Report)Australian Bureau of Statistics 2019, Labour force, Australia: May 2019 (Catalogue No. 6202.0), ABS, Canberra.
м»ЁнҚјлҹ°мҠӨ л…јл¬ё(Conference Paper)Jakubowicz, A. 2002, вҖҳRace vilification and communal leadershipвҖҷ, Beyond Tolerance: National Conference on Racism, Human Rights and Equal Opportunities Commission, Sydney, viewed 2 Sep 2009, <http://www.humanrights.gov.au/...>
мң нҠңлёҢ/мҳҒмғҒ(YouTube/Video)UTS Library 2009, ItвҖҷs the UTS Library with Mr Hank, videorecording, YouTube, viewed 21 Sep 2009, <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QYP_hZmcRgg>

нӢҖлҰ¬кё° мү¬мҡҙ мЈјмҡ” к·ңм№ҷвҖӢ

  • м Җмһҗ 4лӘ… мқҙмғҒ: ліёл¬ём—” мІ« м Җмһҗ et al., Reference Listм—” лӘЁл‘җ мһ‘м„ұ
  • м¶ңнҢҗм—°лҸ„ м—ҶмқҢ: n.d. мӮ¬мҡ© (мҳҲ: Brown n.d.)
  • м§Ғм ‘ мқёмҡ© нҺҳмқҙм§Җ: (Kim 2018, p. 22), (Kim 2018, pp. 22вҖ“24)
  • кё°кҙҖ/лӢЁмІҙ м Җмһҗ: (Australian Bureau of Statistics 2019)
  • мӣ№мӮ¬мқҙнҠё: вҖҳviewed лӮ м§ңвҖҷмҷҖ URL н•„мҲҳ
  • мһҗлЈҢмң нҳ•лі„ нҸ¬л§· м—„мҲҳ

Citation Style LanguageвҖӢ

  • Harvard University of Technology Sydney

item.title: м—°кө¬л№„/н”„лЎңм қнҠё м ңм•Ҳм„ң, Executive Summary & Project Proposal
item.content:encoded:
н”„лЎңм қнҠё м ңм•Ҳм„ңВ·м—°кө¬л№„ м§Җмӣҗм„ңВ·к·ёлһңнҠё(Grant) мһ‘м„ұм—җм„ң Executive SummaryлҠ” к°ҖмһҘ мӨ‘мҡ”н•Ё

Executive Summary к°ңл…җвҖӢ

  • м „мІҙ м ңм•Ҳм„ңмқҳ вҖңмҳҒнҷ” мҳҲкі нҺёвҖқ м—ӯн• 
  • 2-4лӢЁлқҪ, 200-400лӢЁм–ҙ мқҙлӮҙ
  • мқҳмӮ¬кІ°м •мһҗВ·нӣ„мӣҗмһҗВ·мӢ¬мӮ¬мһҗм—җкІҢ вҖңмҷң мқҙ м ңм•Ҳмқ„ м§Җмӣҗн•ҙм•ј н•ҳлҠ”к°ҖвҖқлҘј м§§кі  к°•л ¬н•ҳкІҢ м„Өл“қ
  • м •ліҙ + мҳҒк°җ(м„Өл“қ) мқҙ лӘЁл‘җ нҸ¬н•Ёлҗҳм–ҙм•ј н•Ё (л¬ҙм—Үмқ„/мҷң/м–ҙл–»кІҢ/мҡ°лҰ¬л§Ңмқҳ к°•м җ/л¬ҙм—Үмқ„ мҡ”мІӯн•ҳлҠ”к°Җ)

Executive Summary н•„мҲҳ мҡ”мҶҢвҖӢ

  1. Who you are
    • мЎ°м§Ғ/нҢҖмқҳ м •мІҙм„ұ, лҜём…ҳ, м „л¬ём„ұ
  2. What you do
    • н•өмӢ¬ мӮ¬м—…В·н”„лЎңм қнҠё(мқҙлІҲ м ңм•Ҳмқҳ кіЁмһҗ)
  3. Problem
    • н•ҙкІ°н•ҳл ӨлҠ” н•өмӢ¬ мқҙмҠҲ, мӮ¬нҡҢм Ғ/кіјн•ҷм Ғ н•„мҡ”м„ұ
  4. How
    • м–ҙл–»кІҢ н•ҙкІ°н•  кІғмқёк°Җ? (л°©лІ•, м„ңл№„мҠӨ, м „лһө)
  5. What makes you unique
    • м°Ёлі„м„ұ, кё°мЎҙкіјмқҳ м°Ёмқҙ, мӢӨм ҒВ·мҲҳм№ҳВ·мһ„нҢ©нҠё
  6. Request/Need
    • н•„мҡ”н•ң мһҗмӣҗ, мҳҲмӮ°, нҳ‘л Ҙ л“ұ
  7. Call to Action
    • мӢ¬мӮ¬мһҗ/нӣ„мӣҗмһҗм—җкІҢ вҖңм§Җмӣҗ/нҳ‘м—…вҖқмқ„ мң лҸ„н•ҳлҠ” л§Ҳл¬ҙлҰ¬

мһ‘м„ұмҳҲмӢңвҖӢ

1лӢЁлқҪвҖӢ

2~3л¬ёмһҘмңјлЎң мһҗм—°мҠӨлҹҪкІҢ нҸ¬н•Ё

  • лҜём…ҳ/м •мІҙм„ұ(Who),
  • н•өмӢ¬ лӮҙмҡ©/н”„лЎңк·ёлһЁ(What),
  • мҡ”мІӯмӮ¬н•ӯ(Request)
  • м°Ёлі„м„ұ(Why us?)

2лӢЁлқҪвҖӢ

  • м ңм•Ҳ/н”„лЎңм қнҠёк°Җ м–ҙл–Ө л¬ём ң, лҢҖмғҒ, м§Җм—ӯм—җ мҙҲм җмқ„ л‘җлҠ”м§Җ
  • м„ұмһҘВ·м„ұкіјВ·мһ„нҢ©нҠё л“ұ кө¬мІҙ лҚ°мқҙн„°
  • нҳ„мһ¬ м§Җмӣҗмқҙ мҷң н•„мҡ”н•ңм§Җ, кө¬мІҙм ҒмңјлЎң м„ӨлӘ…

3лӢЁлқҪвҖӢ

  • нҠ№лі„н•Ё, мӢӨм Ғ, м¶”мІңмӮ¬В·нҶөкі„В·к°қкҙҖм Ғ лҚ°мқҙн„°
  • мӢ¬мӮ¬мһҗВ·нӣ„мӣҗмһҗлҘј н–үлҸҷн•ҳкІҢ н•ҳлҠ” мҪңнҲ¬м•Ўм…ҳ(Call to action)
    • (вҖңм§ҖкёҲ н•Ёк»ҳ н•ҙлӢ¬лқјвҖқ, вҖңмқҙ м§Җмӣҗмқҙ н•„мҡ”н•ҳлӢӨвҖқ л“ұ)

мһ‘м„ұ нҢҒвҖӢ

  • л„Ҳл¬ҙ м¶”мғҒм Ғмқҙкұ°лӮҳ м •ліҙл§Ң лӮҳм—ҙн•ҳм§Җ м•Ҡкё°
  • мһ„нҢ©нҠё мһҲлҠ” мҲҳм№ҳВ·кІ°кіјВ·м¶”мІңмӮ¬ м Ғк·№ нҷңмҡ©
  • мӢ¬мӮ¬мһҗ(нӣ„мӣҗмһҗ)мқҳ кҙҖм җм—җм„ң вҖңмҷң м§ҖкёҲ, мҷң мҡ°лҰ¬вҖқлҘј лӢөн•  кІғ
  • ліёл¬ё(н”„лЎңнҸ¬м Ҳ) м „мІҙ лӮҙмҡ©кіј мқјм№ҳ, м—°кІ°лҗҳм–ҙм•ј н•Ё
  • мөңмў… мһ‘м„ұ нӣ„ лҸҷлЈҢ/л©ҳнҶ м—җкІҢ н”јл“ңл°ұ кјӯ л°ӣкё°

нҸүк°Җкё°мӨҖвҖӢ

н•ӯлӘ©л°°м җм„ёл¶Җ нҸүк°Җ кё°мӨҖ
Cover Page and Table of Contents2м§Җм •лҗң лӘЁл“  м •ліҙ(м–‘мӢқ, лӘ©м°Ё л“ұ)лҘј м •нҷ•нһҲ нҸ¬н•Ён•ҳкі  мһҲмқҢ
Executive Summary6м ңм•Ҳм„ңлҘј л°”нғ•мңјлЎң, к°„кІ°н•ҳл©ҙм„ңлҸ„ мҷ„м „н•ң мҡ”м•Ҫкіј н•©лҰ¬м Ғмқё к¶Ңкі (м ңм•Ҳ)к°Җ м ңмӢңлҗҳм–ҙ мһҲмқҢ
Introduction5м—°кө¬ л¬ём ң, м—°кө¬мқҳ л§ҘлқҪ, лӘ©н‘ң, мһ‘м„ұмһҗ кҙҖм җмқҙ лӘ…нҷ•нһҲ м§„мҲ лҗЁ.
м—°кө¬мқҳ мӨ‘мҡ”м„ұкіј кҙҖл Ём„ұмқҙ лӘ…нҷ•нһҲ м „лӢ¬лҗЁ.
ліҙкі м„ңмқҳ мЈјмҡ” кІ°кіјк°Җ к°„лӢЁнһҲ м–ёкёүлҗЁ. ліҙкі м„ңмқҳ м „мІҙ кө¬м„ұм—җ лҢҖн•ң к°„лһөн•ң м•ҲлӮҙк°Җ м ңкіөлҗЁ.
Research Aims and Objectives5мқҙн•ҙн•ҳкё° мүҪкі  мӢӨнҳ„ к°ҖлҠҘн•ң м—°кө¬ лӘ©н‘ңмҷҖ лӘ©м Ғмқҙ м ңмӢңлҗЁ.
м—°кө¬ л¬ём ңмқҳ лӘ…нҷ•м„ұкіј м—°кө¬ н”„лЎңм қнҠёлЎңм„ңмқҳ мӢӨн–ү к°ҖлҠҘм„ұмқҙ мһҳ л“ңлҹ¬лӮЁ.
Background6н•ҙлӢ№ мЈјм ңм—җ лҢҖн•ң кё°мЎҙ м§ҖмӢқмқ„ л…јлҰ¬м Ғмқҙкі  л№„нҢҗм ҒмңјлЎң мў…н•©н•Ё.
л°°кІҪ м„ӨлӘ…мқҙ м—°кө¬ л¬ём ңмҷҖ м§Ғм ‘м ҒмңјлЎң м—°кҙҖлҗҳм–ҙ мһҲмқҢ.
Research Significance and Innovation6м—°кө¬мқҳ мӨ‘мҡ”м„ұмқҙ лӘ…нҷ•нһҲ м§„мҲ лҗЁ. м—°кө¬ кІ°кіјлЎң мқҙл“қмқ„ м–»лҠ” лҢҖмғҒмқҙ кө¬мІҙм ҒмңјлЎң м ңмӢңлҗЁ.
м—°кө¬мқҳ нҳҒмӢ м„ұ/мғҲлЎңмӣҖмқҙ л¶„лӘ…нһҲ кё°мҲ лҗЁ.
л°°кІҪ(л¬ён—Ңкі м°°)кіј м—°кі„н•ҙ мҷң мқҙ л¬ём ңк°Җ м—°кө¬лҗҳм–ҙм•ј н•ҳлҠ”м§Җ м„Өл“қл Ҙ мһҲкІҢ л…јмҰқн•Ё.
Research Methods6м„ нғқн•ң м—°кө¬ л°©лІ•мқҙ лӘ…нҷ•нһҲ м„ӨлӘ…лҗҳкі , м Ғм Ҳн•ң м°ёкі  л¬ён—Ң(к·јкұ°)лЎң л’·л°ӣм№ЁлҗЁ.
м„ нғқн•ң м—°кө¬ л°©лІ•кіј м—°кө¬ л¬ём ң л°Ҹ лӘ©н‘ң к°„мқҳ м—°кІ°мқҙ лӘ…нҷ•нһҲ л“ңлҹ¬лӮЁ.
Conclusion5н”„лЎңм қнҠё м ңм•Ҳм„ң м „мІҙмқҳ л…јлҰ¬м Ғ мҡ”м•Ҫмқ„ м ңкіөн•Ё.
м—°кө¬ л¬ём ңмқҳ мӨ‘мҡ”м„ұкіј, м ңм•Ҳлҗң м—°кө¬ м ‘к·јлІ•мқҳ нҡЁкіјм„ұмқ„ м„Өл“қл Ҙ мһҲкІҢ кІ°лЎ м§ҖмқҢ.
Reference List4Harvard мҠӨнғҖмқјм—җ л§һм¶ҳ мҷ„лІҪн•ң м°ёкі л¬ён—Ң лҰ¬мҠӨнҠёк°Җ мһ‘м„ұлҗЁ.

item.title: мҙҲлЎқ мһ‘м„ұ, Writing Abstracts
item.content:encoded:
• мҙҲлЎқ(Abstract)мқҖ л…јл¬ёВ·лҰ¬нҸ¬нҠёВ·н•ҷмң„л…јл¬ём—җм„ң к°ҖмһҘ лЁјм Җ мқҪнһҲлҠ” н•өмӢ¬ мҡ”м•Ҫл¬ё
  • л§ҺмқҖ лҸ…мһҗк°Җ мҙҲлЎқл§Ң ліҙкі  ліёл¬ёмқ„ мқҪмқ„м§Җ кІ°м •н•ҳкё° л•Ңл¬ём—җ, м§§м§Җл§Ң мҷ„м„ұлҸ„ лҶ’кі  м •ліҙ л°ҖлҸ„к°Җ лҶ’мқҖ кёҖмқ„ мҚЁм•јн•Ё

мҙҲлЎқ м—ӯн• вҖӢ

  • лҸ…лҰҪм Ғ(stand-alone) н…ҚмҠӨнҠёлЎң, 100300лӢЁм–ҙ(н•ҷмҲ м§Җ/м»ЁнҚјлҹ°мҠӨлҠ” 120150лӢЁм–ҙ лӮҙмҷё)
  • л…јл¬ё м „мІҙмқҳ мҠӨлғ…мғ·
  • мҙҲлЎқл§Ң мқҪкі лҸ„ "л¬ҙм—Үмқ„/мҷң/м–ҙл–»кІҢ/л¬ҙм—Үмқ„ л°ңкІ¬/мҷң мӨ‘мҡ”н•ңм§Җ"лҘј м•Ң мҲҳ мһҲм–ҙм•ј н•Ё

мҙҲлЎқмқҳ н•өмӢ¬ нҸ¬н•Ё мҡ”мҶҢ(мҲңм„ң & м§Ҳл¬ё)вҖӢ

  1. Background: л°°кІҪ м •ліҙ, м—°кө¬к°Җ лӢӨлЈЁлҠ” мқјл°ҳм Ғ л¬ём ң(нҳ„мһ¬ мӢңм ң)
  2. Purpose: м—°кө¬ лӘ©м Ғ, мЈјмҡ” м§Ҳл¬ё(кіјкұ°/нҳ„мһ¬мҷ„лЈҢ мӢңм ң)
  3. Methodology: м—°кө¬ л°©лІ•, лҚ°мқҙн„°(кіјкұ° мӢңм ң)
  4. Results: н•өмӢ¬ кІ°кіј(кіјкұ° мӢңм ң)
  5. Conclusion/Significance: кІ°лЎ  лҳҗлҠ” мӢңмӮ¬м җ, м¶”мІңмӮ¬н•ӯ(нҳ„мһ¬ мӢңм ң лҳҗлҠ” м¶”лЎ /мЎ°мӢ¬мҠӨлҹ¬мҡҙ мЎ°лҸҷмӮ¬)

н•өмӢ¬ м§Ҳл¬ёвҖӢ

  • What did you do? (л¬ҙм—Үмқ„ н–ҲлӮҳ?)
  • Why did you do it? (мҷң н–ҲлӮҳ?)
  • How did you do it? (м–ҙл–»кІҢ н–ҲлӮҳ?)
  • What did you find? (л¬ҙм—Үмқ„ л°ңкІ¬н–ҲлӮҳ?)
  • Why does it matter? (мҷң мӨ‘мҡ”н•ңк°Җ?)

мҙҲлЎқмқҳ м–ём–ҙм Ғ нҠ№м§• & л¬ёмһҘ кө¬м„ұвҖӢ

  • м •ліҙ л°ҖлҸ„ лҶ’кІҢ, к°„кІ°н•ҳкі  кө¬мІҙм ҒмңјлЎң
  • мқҪкё° мү¬мҡҙ кө¬м„ұ (мӢңмһ‘-мӨ‘к°„-лҒқмқҙ мһҗм—°мҠӨлҹҪкІҢ мқҙм–ҙм§җ)
  • мһҗкё°мҷ„кІ°м„ұ: м•Ҫм–ҙ/кі мң м–ҙ м •мқҳ, мқёмҡ©В·м°ёмЎ° м—Ҷмқҙ мқҙн•ҙ к°ҖлҠҘ
  • нӮӨмӣҢл“ң н’Қл¶Җн•ҳкІҢ нҸ¬н•Ё
  • к°қкҙҖм Ғ/мӮ¬мӢӨ мӨ‘мӢ¬ м„ңмҲ , нҸүк°ҖлҠ” мөңмҶҢ
  • лҸҷмӮ¬ мӨ‘мӢ¬, лӘ…мӮ¬В·нҳ•мҡ©мӮ¬В·л¶ҖмӮ¬ м¶•мҶҢ
  • мӨ‘мҡ”н•ң м •ліҙл¶Җн„° л¬ёмһҘ л§Ё м•һм—җ
  • л¬ёлІ•/мӢңм ң к·ңм№ҷ
    • л°°кІҪ: нҳ„мһ¬мӢңм ң
    • лӘ©м Ғ/мЈјмҡ”нҷңлҸҷ: кіјкұ° or нҳ„мһ¬мҷ„лЈҢ
    • л°©лІ•/кІ°кіј: кіјкұ°
    • кІ°лЎ /мқҳмқҳ: нҳ„мһ¬мӢңм ң, м¶”лЎ /мЎ°лҸҷмӮ¬(should, may л“ұ)

к°„кІ°н•ң мҙҲлЎқ мһ‘м„ұ нҢҒвҖӢ

  • лӘЁл“  мҲ«мһҗлҠ” л¬ёмһҘ мІ«лЁёлҰ¬к°Җ м•„лӢҲл©ҙ мҲ«мһҗ(1, 2, 3...)лЎң н‘ңкё°
  • к°ҖлҠҘн•ң н•ң лҠҘлҸҷнғң(active voice) мӮ¬мҡ©, 1мқём№ӯмқҖ м§Җм–‘
  • лҸҷмӮ¬лЎң н•өмӢ¬мқ„ м „лӢ¬, лӘ…мӮ¬кө¬В·мҲҳмӢқм–ҙ м¶•мҶҢ
  • "at this point in time" вҶ’ "now" / "on a yearly basis" вҶ’ "yearly" л“ұмңјлЎң м§§кІҢ
  • мқҳлҜё м—ҶлҠ” мҲҳмӢқм–ҙВ·мӨ‘ліө н‘ңнҳ„ мӮӯм ң
  • л¶Ҳн•„мҡ”н•ң нҸүк°Җ/мқјл°ҳнҷ” л¬ёмһҘ н”јн•ҳкё°
  • мҷёл¶Җ мқёмҡ©л¬ё/м°ёмЎ°(нҠ№нһҲ м„ңм§Җ)лҠ” кұ°мқҳ мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳм§Җ м•ҠмқҢ
  • кҙҖлЎҖ/к·ңм •мқҖ мҶҢмҶҚ л¶„м•ј лҳҗлҠ” н•ҷнҡҢ/м Җл„җ мҳҲмӢң м°ёкі 

мҳҲмӢң: мҙҲлЎқмқҳ м „нҳ•м Ғ кө¬мЎ°мҷҖ мӢңм ңвҖӢ

мҡ”мҶҢ(мҲңм„ң)мӢңм ңмҳҲмӢң
Backgroundнҳ„мһ¬мӢңм ңType A behavior is...
Purposeкіјкұ°/нҳ„мһ¬мҷ„лЈҢIn this study we evaluated...
Methodкіјкұ°мӢңм ңChildren performed a 5-trial task.
Resultsкіјкұ°мӢңм ңResults showed...
Conclusionнҳ„мһ¬/мЎ°лҸҷмӮ¬The results suggest that...

мІҙнҒ¬лҰ¬мҠӨнҠёвҖӢ

  • Background, Purpose, Method, Results, Conclusion/Significance лӘЁл‘җ нҸ¬н•Ё?
  • л¶Ҳн•„мҡ”н•ң лӢЁм–ҙлӮҳ мӨ‘ліө мҲҳмӢқм–ҙк°Җ м—ҶлҠ”к°Җ?
  • нӮӨмӣҢл“ңлҠ” м¶©л¶„нһҲ л“Өм–ҙмһҲлҠ”к°Җ?
  • мҙҲлЎқл§Ң мқҪм–ҙлҸ„ л…јл¬ёмқҳ нқҗлҰ„(мӢңмһ‘-мӨ‘к°„-лҒқ)мқҙ мһҗм—°мҠӨлҹ¬мҡҙк°Җ?
  • лҸ…лҰҪм ҒмңјлЎң мқҙн•ҙлҗ  мҲҳ мһҲлҠ”к°Җ? (мҷёл¶Җ мқёмҡ©, м•Ҫм–ҙ лҜём •мқҳ л“ұ л¬ём ң м—ҶмқҢ?)
  • нҸүк°Җ/к°җм •м Ғ н‘ңнҳ„ м—Ҷмқҙ, мӮ¬мӢӨ мӨ‘мӢ¬мңјлЎң мҡ”м•Ҫлҗҳм–ҙ мһҲлҠ”к°Җ?
  • н•ҷнҡҢ/м Җл„җмқҳ мҙҲлЎқ л¶„лҹү, мһ‘м„ұ к·ңм№ҷмқ„ л”°лһҗлҠ”к°Җ?

мҙҲлЎқ мҳҲмӢң(нӢҖ)вҖӢ

  • л°°кІҪ: мЈјм ң, л¬ём ң м„ӨлӘ…
  • лӘ©м Ғ: м—°кө¬ лӘ©м Ғ, м§Ҳл¬ё
  • л°©лІ•: лҚ°мқҙн„°, мӢӨн—ҳ, мЎ°мӮ¬лІ• л“ұ
  • кІ°кіј: мЈјмҡ” кІ°кіј
  • мқҳмқҳ: кІ°лЎ , мӢӨл¬ҙм Ғ/н•ҷл¬ём Ғ мҳҒн–Ҙ, н•ңкі„/лҜёлһҳ м—°кө¬

кІ°лЎ вҖӢ

  • лӮҙ л¶„м•јмқҳ м—¬лҹ¬ л…јл¬ё мҙҲлЎқмқ„ м§Ғм ‘ мқҪкі , м •ліҙ л°°м№ҳ/м–ём–ҙ/кёёмқҙлҘј нҢҢм•…н•  кІғ
  • мҙҲлЎқмқҖ л§Ҳм§Җл§үм—җ кёүн•ҳкІҢ м“°м§Җ л§җкі , ліёл¬ёліҙлӢӨ лҚ” мӢ кІҪ мҚЁм„ң м—¬лҹ¬ лІҲ лӢӨл“¬мқ„ кІғ
  • л°ҳл“ңмӢң м—°кө¬мқҳ "мғҲлЎңмӣҖ, мқҳмқҳ, м°Ёлі„м„ұ"мқ„ н•ңл‘җ л¬ёмһҘмңјлЎң лӘ…нҷ•нһҲ л“ңлҹ¬лӮј кІғ
